2025 · arXiv · Agents

UpBench: A Dynamically Evolving Real-World Labor-Market Agentic Benchmark Framework Built for Human-Centric AI

322 real, paid Upwork jobs turned into a benchmark — each decomposed by an expert freelancer into 5–20 verifiable acceptance criteria, with per-criterion human feedback. Then it asks a question most agent benchmarks skip: when the agent fails, how much does a single round of human feedback rescue?

Yi, Liu, Terzolo, Hasson, Sinha, Mendes, Rabinovich · Upwork
venue arXiv citations 3 topic Agents source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-06-20
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

Agent benchmarks have a realism problem. Most are static, synthetic, or domain-limited: a fixed test set of puzzles, code katas, or simulated web stores that tells you little about whether a model can actually do the kind of work people pay for. The economically grounded benchmarks that do exist — OpenAI's GDPval (44 occupations) and Mercor's APEX (banking, consulting, law, medicine) — moved toward real professional tasks, but UpBench argues they still tend to score single-turn deliverables against a proxy or an answer key, rather than the full, feedback-rich workflow of a real engagement.

UpBench's sibling on the Upwork data, SWE-Lancer, showed real freelance software jobs could anchor an eval; the Remote Labor Index (RLI) generalized that to multi-sector paid remote work. UpBench pushes on three axes those leave open: (1) graded, per-criterion evaluation instead of binary pass/fail, authored by domain-expert freelancers; (2) human-in-the-loop re-attempts, so the benchmark measures not just autonomy but responsiveness to feedback; and (3) a continuously refreshed task stream drawn from a live marketplace, as a defense against the saturation and contamination that eventually kill any static benchmark.

The framing is deliberately human-centric: the goal is to measure how AI augments a freelancer's work — a centaur setup — not just whether it can replace one. That makes the per-criterion human feedback traces, not just the leaderboard, the artifact the authors care about.

// how it works

The method

Every task in UpBench is a verified, completed Upwork contract with a real client payout. The first release restricts to fixed-price, single-milestone jobs with clearly defined requirements (hourly work is excluded to avoid scope ambiguity), yielding 322 jobs across eight professional categories — from Web/Mobile/Software Development and Data Science to Translation, Writing, and Admin Support.

The pipeline is built around expert freelancers, not crowd labor. Every recruited expert holds a Top Rated or Top Rated Plus badge with a 100% job-satisfaction score; collectively they have over $1M in cumulative Upwork earnings across ~96,000 platform hours, and are contracted through Upwork itself. They drive four pipeline stages: filtering/curating jobs, labeling them (task completeness, PII), authoring rubrics, and grading deliverables.

Rubrics turn fuzzy job posts into checkable criteria

For each job, an expert decomposes the client's brief into 5–20 acceptance criteria, each labeled by importance: critical (must pass), important (strong alignment), optional (nice-to-have), or pitfall (a condition to avoid, e.g. factual errors or scope drift). This is what lets UpBench grade quality on a gradient rather than a single pass/fail bit — and gives interpretable, per-criterion error analysis.

Two headline metrics

Completion Rate is strict: a job counts as completed only if every critical and important criterion passes — a realistic client-acceptance standard. Rubric Score is graded: the fraction of a job's criteria marked "pass," averaged over jobs, capturing near-misses that a binary metric would throw away.

Rj,k = # criteria passedj,k# criteria totalj,k ∈ [0, 1] // per-job rubric score for job j on attempt k; the dataset-level Rubric Score averages R over all jobs

The agents, and the human-in-the-loop loop

Three minimal agents share one scaffold and differ only in the backing model: Claude Sonnet 4, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and GPT-5. Each runs a simple plan–act–reflect loop (ReAct-style) with lightweight tools to read attachments and store artifacts — deliberately no retrieval, web access, or fine-tuning, to isolate model capability from scaffolding.

Beyond autonomous grading, UpBench adds a human-in-the-loop (HITL) condition. When an agent's first attempt (k=1) fails, an expert returns per-criterion pass/fail plus textual feedback; the agent revises, and the new submission (k=2) is re-graded. This directly measures responsiveness to feedback and self-correction — the share of failed jobs a single human pass can "rescue."

// what they found

The results

Even with a bare-bones scaffold, the spread across models is large. On first attempt (P1), Claude Sonnet 4 completes 39.8% of jobs to the strict critical-plus-important standard — roughly double Gemini 2.5 Pro (19.9%) and GPT-5 (19.6%). Adding one human-feedback pass lifts every model: overall completion rises to 0.512 / 0.323 / 0.335 respectively, an absolute gain of 11–14 percentage points (relative gains of 29% for Claude up to 71% for GPT-5). The rescue rate — the fraction of initially failed jobs recovered after one HITL iteration — lands at 18–23%: nearly one in five failures salvaged.

AgentP1 (k=1)P overallAbs liftRel liftRescue
Claude Sonnet 40.3980.512+0.11528.9%23.3%
Gemini 2.5 Pro0.1990.323+0.12462.5%18.0%
GPT-50.1960.335+0.14071.4%19.0%

The graded Rubric Score tells a softer story underneath the strict completion bar. On the full cohort, average rubric scores rise modestly — Claude 0.26→0.33, Gemini 0.30→0.34, GPT-5 0.27→0.36 (absolute lift 0.06–0.09 on a 0–1 scale). But on the subset of jobs that initially failed, HITL helps far more: Claude 0.16→0.29, Gemini 0.21→0.26, GPT-5 0.16→0.27 — relative rubric gains of roughly 40–70%. In other words, human feedback does the most work exactly where the agent was stuck.

An economic lens on when to use AI, HITL, or a human

Because each job carries its real payout, UpBench models the expected net value of each approach as success rate times task value minus cost, E(Vi) = si·V − ci. Setting two approaches equal gives a breakeven task value:

V* = cH − cLsH − sL // above V*, the higher-success / higher-cost method (e.g. HITL) wins; below it, the cheaper one does

This yields three regimes: AI-only is optimal for low-value work where accuracy matters little against cost; HITL overtakes it for mid-value tasks where added human cost is justified by higher success; and human-only wins for high-value, high-risk jobs where the cost of failure dominates. The authors stress these thresholds are illustrative, data-driven approximations that shift with model quality and labor cost — not universal constants.

A scale analysis of all rubric criteria (embedded with text-embedding-3-large, hierarchically clustered, then GPT-5-labeled into 20 themes) shows where agents stumble: "deliverable format and content requirements" is both the most common criterion type (18.45%) and the most-failed (15.49%), with spreadsheet column/row requirements (9.37% of failures) and financial-report structure (7.48%) close behind. Much of the gap is precise formatting and structured-output compliance, not deep reasoning.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

UpBench's design is genuinely strong where it counts — real paid jobs, expert-authored rubrics, and per-criterion human feedback are a real step up from binary, synthetic agent evals. But the specific numbers come with caveats the paper is admirably upfront about, and the flagship claim deserves scrutiny.

The biggest one: the marquee "+11–14 pp from human feedback" conflates the value of feedback with the value of a second try. The authors explicitly did not run a matched AI-only k=2 control (re-attempt the same failed jobs with no human input), so the HITL lift cannot be cleanly attributed to human guidance versus a plain retry. Layer on that the k=3 completion bars for Gemini (41.1%) and GPT-5 (42.3%) are model-predicted, not measured, and the upper end of the "HITL keeps helping" curve is partly extrapolation.

Grading reliability is the other soft spot. Each criterion gets a single expert's binary pass/fail; the paper reports no inter-rater agreement and no confidence intervals, so the per-model deltas (some only ~1 pp apart on rescue rate) sit on unmeasured noise. And the strict all-or-nothing completion definition means a 0.512 completion rate coexists with a ~0.33 rubric score — most "failures" are partial successes the headline metric hides.

baselines

Only three frontier models, all on a deliberately minimal plan–act–reflect scaffold with no tools, retrieval, or web. So the absolute numbers (Claude 39.8%, others ~20%) are a floor, not a frontier — tool-rich agents would likely score higher. No human-only arm is actually run on the same jobs; the human curve is modeled.

contamination

Deliverables and client communications are withheld from graders and the dataset, which is good. But Upwork job posts are public and may sit in training data, and the paper doesn't test for solution memorization. The "continuously refreshed" stream is the real defense — yet the released 322-job snapshot is static.

what's missed

No AI-only second-attempt control to isolate feedback from retry; k=3 numbers for two of three models are predicted, not observed; single grader with no IRR or CIs; 322 jobs, fixed-price/single-milestone only, sampled for feasibility rather than to mirror the marketplace.

would raise confidence

Add a matched AI-only k=2 control; multiple independent graders with reported inter-rater reliability and CIs; a real human-only arm on the same jobs; tool-augmented agent baselines; representative + harder sampling (hourly, multi-milestone); and a contamination probe on public job posts.

// bottom line

A real advance in what agent evals measure — paid work, graded rubrics, human feedback in the loop, built to refresh. As released, though, the specific results are conservative, noisy estimates, and the headline human-in-the-loop gain isn't yet cleanly separable from "just try again." The most durable artifact may not be the leaderboard but the per-criterion feedback traces, which are explicitly built for RLHF and feedback-driven agent training.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. UpBench grades agents on 322 real, paid Upwork jobs using expert-authored rubrics (5–20 criteria, labeled critical/important/optional/pitfall) with per-criterion human feedback — a graded, human-centric alternative to binary synthetic agent benchmarks.
  2. On strict completion, Claude Sonnet 4 (39.8%) roughly doubles Gemini and GPT-5 (~20%) with a minimal, tool-free scaffold; a single human-feedback pass adds 11–14 pp and rescues 18–23% of failed jobs.
  3. The HITL lift lacks an AI-only retry control, two of three k=3 numbers are model-predicted, and grading is single-rater with no inter-rater agreement — read the deltas as directional, not definitive.
  4. Failures cluster on format and structured-output compliance (deliverable format 15.5%, spreadsheets 9.4%), not deep reasoning — and the embedded economic model frames a concrete AI → HITL → human handoff as task value rises.
